host_validation_envelope.claim: Course tooling compatibility only; this is not an Orange supported-host claim.

orange_source.revision_context: Canonical merged main revision closing the accepted pre-alpha S3a evidence; this is not an Orange release.

statuses.released: Complete learner material exists and passes all release gates.
statuses.planned: Graph position and competency intent exist, but learner material is not released.
statuses.blocked: Release is forbidden until every named blocker is resolved.

source_maturities.general: Prerequisite knowledge that makes no Orange feature claim.
source_maturities.implemented: Behavior exists and is tested at the pinned Orange revision.
source_maturities.ratified: Normatively decided but not implemented at the pinned revision.
source_maturities.proposed: Directional Orange design that may change.

journeys.name: Install and initialize an exact toolchain
Import a standard and author a specification
Update, deprecate, withdraw, or replace a profile
Respond to a vulnerability or invalidated claim

competencies.description: Use terminals, files, processes, and exact commands safely and reproducibly.
Use version control, tests, debugging, and CI to investigate and communicate changes.
Design programs with functions, control flow, data, and explicit state.
Use types, explicit errors, tests, and debugging to build reliable software.
Select and analyze data structures and algorithms using resource bounds.
Read and write the C and Rust needed for systems and foreign-interface work.
Use logic, definitions, induction, contradiction, and constructive proof techniques.
Reason about number theory, modular arithmetic, algebraic structures, and finite fields.
Model discrete probability, randomness, expectation, and statistical evidence.
State cryptographic goals, attacker models, assumptions, and security notions.
Analyze primitives, constructions, protocols, failure behavior, and misuse resistance.
Trace standards, errata, parameters, provenance, and canonical test vectors.
Reason about representation, fixed-width arithmetic, layout, memory, and ownership.
Inspect object files, calling conventions, ABIs, targets, SIMD, linking, and dispatch.
Define leakage models and assess constant-time and side-channel evidence honestly.
Explain lexing, parsing, syntax trees, recovery, diagnostics, and resource bounds.
Reason with operational semantics, name resolution, type systems, and effects.
Trace compiler IRs, lowering, optimization, code generation, and preservation evidence.
Use specifications, contracts, invariants, termination arguments, and refinement.
Distinguish proof checking from proof search and inspect terms, certificates, axioms, and automation.
Model probabilistic games, adversary interfaces, reductions, and cryptographic assumptions.
Write scoped claims and enumerate evidence bases, assumptions, exclusions, and trusted computing bases.
Design positive, negative, differential, fuzz, mutation, and adversarial tests.
Build deterministic, content-addressed, inspectable, offline-replayable evidence.
Operate support, updates, deprecation, revocation, and vulnerability response without claim drift.
Install, identify, and use an exact supported Orange toolchain without overstating its capability.
Predict and inspect Orange 2026 lexical behavior and stable lexical diagnostics.
Author and test the complete implemented Orange 2026 grammar and conformance boundary.
Import an exact standard and author an admitted Orange specification.
Implement a specification, discharge obligations, and classify each Orange claim independently.
Build and inspect a supported Orange native artifact and its preservation chain.
Integrate an Orange artifact through generated C and Rust interfaces and validate boundary failures.
Audit hostile evidence input and replay an Orange claim bundle offline.
Update, migrate, deprecate, withdraw, or replace an Orange profile with traceable claim impact.
Contain and remediate a vulnerability or invalidated Orange claim.
Independently deliver and defend cryptographic implementation evidence as P-01.
Independently deliver and defend proof and trust-closure evidence as P-02.
Independently deliver and defend standards-to-specification evidence as P-03.
Independently deliver and defend integration and lifecycle evidence as P-04.
Independently deliver and defend offline audit evidence as P-05.

modules.title: Start here: evidence, status, and your path
modules.summary: Learn how Orange School works, classify evidence honestly, and choose a placement path.

outcomes.text: Distinguish released, planned, and blocked curriculum material.
Classify a test, proof, assumption, and proposal without strengthening its claim.
Produce a personal path that begins at the earliest unpassed assessment.

modules.title: Terminal, files, and repeatable commands
modules.summary: Navigate a filesystem, run bounded commands, read exit status, and record reproducible evidence.

outcomes.text: Navigate and inspect a workspace without relying on a graphical file browser.
Run a command, separate standard output from diagnostics, and interpret its exit status.
Write a reproducible command record with working directory, inputs, command, and observed result.

modules.title: Git and reproducible change history
modules.summary: Use commits, branches, diffs, and exact revisions as evidence.

outcomes.text: Inspect a repository's exact HEAD, worktree status, and diff without changing it.
Create focused Git history in a fresh temporary repository.
Produce a reproducible evidence record tied to an exact revision and diff.

modules.title: Tests, debugging, automation, and CI
modules.summary: Reproduce failures, isolate causes, automate checks, and read CI evidence.

outcomes.text: Reproduce a failure and isolate the smallest input or condition that triggers it.
Design and run tests with unambiguous pass or fail exit behavior.
Interpret a CI check record without extending its result beyond the tested scope.

modules.title: Programming from first principles
modules.summary: Build programs from expressions, functions, control, data, and explicit state.

outcomes.text: Trace expressions, variables, functions, and control flow for a concrete input.
Write a Python program with explicit input, output, and state.
Decompose a problem and test normal, boundary, and invalid cases.

modules.title: Data structures, algorithms, and bounds
modules.summary: Choose representations and reason about time, space, and bounded work.

outcomes.text: Choose and justify list, tuple, dictionary, and set representations.
Implement and test search, count, deduplication, and ordering algorithms.
State and enforce time, space, and input bounds for an algorithm.

modules.title: Types, errors, tests, and debugging
modules.summary: Make failures explicit and build evidence that code handles them.

outcomes.text: Use type annotations and explicit invariants to define accepted inputs and outputs.
Turn a reproduced failure into a focused unit test and diagnose it systematically.
Design explicit error results that fail closed without hiding context.

modules.title: C and Rust systems bridge
modules.summary: Use C and Rust ownership, build, test, and unsafe-boundary concepts needed for Orange integration.

outcomes.text: Compare C memory, pointer, and bounds contracts with Rust ownership and borrowing using concrete traces.
Build and test bounded C and Rust functions with explicit success and error behavior.
Integrate one C function through a narrow Rust FFI wrapper and document every unsafe assumption.

modules.title: Logic and proof techniques
modules.summary: Read and construct precise definitions, propositions, proofs, and counterexamples.

outcomes.text: Translate precise statements among prose, predicates, quantifiers, truth cases, and counterexamples.
Construct direct, contrapositive, contradiction, and induction arguments.
Distinguish proof from finite testing and enumerate assumptions and scope.

modules.title: Number theory and algebra
modules.summary: Develop the discrete structures underlying modern cryptography.

outcomes.text: Compute and justify gcd, extended Euclid, divisibility, and congruence results.
Analyze closure, identity, inverse, and operation laws in groups, rings, and fields without overclaiming.
Implement and test bounded modular algorithms while separating computation from proof.

modules.title: Modular arithmetic and finite fields
modules.summary: Compute and prove properties of modular and finite-field operations.

outcomes.text: Compute and justify modular reduction, inverses, exponentiation, and finite-prime-field operations.
Implement a bounded prime-field value type with explicit modulus and input invariants and tests.
Use counterexamples to distinguish a prime field from arithmetic modulo a composite and separate tests from proof.

modules.title: Discrete probability and statistical evidence
modules.summary: Reason about random experiments, distributions, bounds, and empirical inference.

outcomes.text: Model finite sample spaces, events, conditional probability, and independence.
Compute expectation plus exact union, collision, and tail bounds for bounded cases.
Implement exact enumeration and a reproducible simulation, then state sampling, error, and pseudorandom limitations.

modules.title: Cryptographic goals and attacker models
modules.summary: Define security goals, capabilities, assumptions, and failure boundaries.

outcomes.text: State an asset-specific cryptographic goal, attacker model, and security-game-style notion with explicit capabilities, resources, exclusions, success event, and evidence limits.
Distinguish keys, nonces, and security randomness while documenting assumptions, trust boundaries, lifetimes, scopes, and failure responses.
Analyze how cryptographic claims compose with encoding, state, key management, and application behavior, then use a bounded evaluator without mistaking structural completeness for security.

modules.title: Symmetric cryptography, hashes, MACs, KDFs, and AEAD
modules.summary: Analyze construction goals, composition rules, misuse, and failure behavior.

outcomes.text: Distinguish symmetric encryption, an unkeyed hash, a MAC, a password KDF, a general key-derivation function, and AEAD by security goal, input contract, output, and limitation.
Analyze nonce uniqueness, tag verification, AAD, domain separation, key separation, and composition rules, including the consequence of violating each invariant.
Use bounded Python 3.11 library mechanics for SHA-256, HMAC-SHA-256, and PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA-256 without presenting the exercise as encryption or production key handling.
Specify and audit a versioned cryptographic manifest, then identify what still requires a maintained production library, deployment authority, and operational review.

modules.title: Public-key cryptography, signatures, and key establishment
modules.summary: Analyze asymmetric primitives, assumptions, protocols, and attack surfaces.

outcomes.text: Distinguish public-key encryption, digital signatures, and key establishment by operation, security goal, attacker capability, and non-goal.
Analyze parameter and key validation, randomness and nonce requirements, public-key authenticity, transcript and context binding, and externally observable failure behavior.

Design and evaluate a KEM-style composition and a classical-to-post-quantum migration boundary using ratified profiles, explicit PKI assumptions, and failure-safe evidence rather than home-grown production cryptography.

modules.title: Standards, errata, provenance, and vectors
modules.summary: Turn exact standards sources into traceable requirements and test evidence.

outcomes.text: Classify a standards source, its exact version and status, normative language, updates, and errata, then extract atomic requirements with stable locations.
Preserve standards and vector provenance with bounded manifests, content digests, review context, and explicit authenticity limits.
Execute positive and negative vectors reproducibly, retain failure evidence, and scope claims to what the vectors actually establish.

modules.title: Representation, arithmetic, and resource bounds
modules.summary: Connect bits and bytes to explicit integer behavior and bounded computation.

outcomes.text: Distinguish mathematical integers from fixed-width unsigned and two's-complement representations, including bits, bytes, hexadecimal, and endian order.
Select and justify checked, wrapping, or saturating arithmetic plus bounded shifts and conversions at representation boundaries.
Encode and parse a bounded binary record with validated length/offset arithmetic and explicit input, time, and memory budgets.

modules.title: Memory, ownership, regions, and zeroization
modules.summary: Reason about allocation, aliasing, lifetime, mutation, and secret erasure.

outcomes.text: Trace C object lifetime, storage duration, allocation, and deallocation, and identify accesses or releases whose behavior is undefined.
Apply Rust ownership, moves, shared and exclusive borrows, slices, aliasing rules, and pointer-provenance obligations to concrete memory regions.
Specify and implement one bounded C-to-Rust ownership transfer with a narrow safe wrapper and a complete unsafe-assumption ledger.
Produce and evaluate zeroization and destruction evidence without claiming that one wipe proves every copy or physical representation was erased.

modules.title: Objects, linking, calling conventions, and ABIs
modules.summary: Inspect native object and foreign-call boundaries down to symbols and layout.

outcomes.text: Trace C17 translation units through relocatable objects, archives, linking, and executable loading; inspect sections, symbols, and relocations while distinguishing C linkage from toolchain visibility.

Specify and measure a target-scoped C/Rust ABI contract for calling convention, exact-width values, field order, size, alignment, padding, and offsets without promoting an observation to a language guarantee.
Design a narrow FFI boundary with explicit pointer, ownership, lifetime, aliasing, mutation, retention, status, output, panic, and unwind rules and a fail-closed safe Rust wrapper.
Produce reproducible, target-specific artifact evidence with capability-detected tools and positive, endpoint, invalid, and deliberate link-failure observations.

modules.title: Targets, SIMD, and feature dispatch
modules.summary: Connect target models and vector operations to exact supported artifact tuples.

outcomes.text: Specify an artifact tuple that distinguishes target triple, ISA, ABI, operating system, object format, endianness, and pointer width, and separate language guarantees from build and inspection observations.
Explain SIMD lane width, lane type, per-lane semantics, alignment, tails, and reductions without inferring semantics from vector width or assuming source notation guarantees vector instructions.
Design a baseline-plus-accelerated dispatch policy that requires an included implementation and runtime capability evidence before entry, and that never executes an unsupported instruction.
Build and inspect reproducible native and cross-target evidence, maintain a capability matrix, and scope every conclusion to the exact compiler, flags, target, and artifact hash observed.

modules.title: Leakage models and side-channel engineering
modules.summary: Separate source discipline, formal leakage claims, artifact inspection, and empirical timing evidence.

outcomes.text: Write an explicit leakage model that identifies the system, adversary, sensitive and public inputs, permitted declassification, observations, target, and claim scope.
Apply source-level control-flow and memory-access discipline, then inspect exact compiled artifacts while accounting for compiler, optimizer, linker, runtime, and microarchitectural effects.
Design a bounded leakage experiment with calibration controls, randomized acquisition, raw effect sizes, Welch-style statistics, confounder handling, and an explicit multiple-testing plan.
Calibrate conclusions from source review, artifact inspection, tests, and timing samples, explaining why each is evidence rather than proof of non-leakage or constant-time behavior.

modules.title: Lexers, parsers, syntax trees, and diagnostics
modules.summary: Build and test deterministic, bounded language frontends.

outcomes.text: Define a byte, Unicode, and source-position policy and build a deterministic bounded lexer with structured diagnostics.
Specify a grammar and precedence, construct a bounded parser, and distinguish concrete syntax from an abstract syntax tree.
Test valid, endpoint, and invalid inputs, limited recovery, diagnostic stability, and resource claims with differential, metamorphic, and deliberate-failure evidence.

modules.title: Semantics, names, types, and effects
modules.summary: Define what programs mean and which invalid behaviors are rejected.

outcomes.text: Define lexical name resolution, binding, and shadowing over a bounded immutable abstract syntax tree.
Derive static type-and-effect judgments that reject invalid programs before evaluation and conservatively describe potential effects.
Specify and execute deterministic call-by-value operational semantics with explicit evaluation order, values, effects, and resource failures.
Test static and dynamic semantics with derivations, endpoints, relational cases, independent oracles, and observed deliberate failures while keeping claims within the model.

modules.title: IRs, lowering, code generation, and preservation
modules.summary: Trace meaning and evidence through a compiler pipeline to artifact bytes.

outcomes.text: Specify a typed IR and lower a bounded source AST while preserving explicit scope, type, single-assignment, and resource invariants.
Validate, serialize, decode, and execute a deterministic bounded artifact with use-before-definition and exact-layout checks.
Establish scoped source-versus-IR equivalence evidence across normal, endpoint, one-beyond, invalid, metamorphic, and deliberate-failure cases without overstating proof or Orange capability.

modules.title: Specifications, contracts, invariants, and refinement
modules.summary: State and prove local obligations connecting intent to implementation behavior.

outcomes.text: Write bounded specifications with explicit assumptions, preconditions, postconditions, old-state references, and structured counterexamples.
Distinguish partial from total correctness and check loop initialization, invariant preservation, exit reasoning, and a decreasing nonnegative variant.
Check finite behavioral refinement by preventing stronger concrete input requirements and requiring concrete results to satisfy the abstract postcondition.
Produce reproducible executable evidence at every exact resource endpoint and smallest constructible overflow while stating what finite checking does not prove.

modules.title: Proof assistants, kernels, and trust
modules.summary: Construct and replay proof objects while tracking axioms and the trusted checker.

outcomes.text: Distinguish propositions, judgments, assumptions, axioms, proof terms, certificates, proof construction, and deterministic replay.
Implement and audit a small trusted kernel whose inference rules, integrity checks, diagnostics, and resource bounds are explicit.
Inventory the trusted computing base and axiom dependencies, then calibrate soundness and theorem claims to the actual evidence.
Validate proof certificates with rule, endpoint, malformed, replay, mutation, deliberate-failure, and recovery evidence.

modules.title: SAT, SMT, automation, and certificates
modules.summary: Use automated search without confusing solver output with checked proof evidence.

outcomes.text: Distinguish automated search from independent result checking and interpret sat, unsat, and unknown without strengthening an unknown result.
Validate bounded CNF inputs, SAT model witnesses, and UNSAT split-tree certificates with stable fail-closed diagnostics.
Enforce explicit search and certificate bounds, then preserve deterministic endpoint, malformed-certificate, deliberate-failure, and restored-pass evidence.
Inventory solver and checker trust, and state only the conditional claims supported by checked certificates.

modules.title: Probabilistic games and reductions
modules.summary: Express game hops, adversaries, advantages, assumptions, and reduction boundaries.

outcomes.text: Specify a bounded probabilistic security game with challenger, adversary, randomness, interfaces, transcript, win event, baseline, advantage, resource bounds, and quantifier order.
Construct reviewable game hops and reductions whose assumptions, bad events, loss terms, and concrete bounds are explicit and directionally valid.
Distinguish proof from exact finite enumeration and seeded simulation, then produce boundary, relational, deliberate-failure, and reproducibility evidence with calibrated claims.

modules.title: Claims, evidence, assumptions, and trusted bases
modules.summary: Produce claim records that say exactly what evidence supports and excludes.

outcomes.text: Write versioned claim records with an exact subject, scope, exclusions, assumptions, and falsifiable language rather than an unbounded quality label.
Classify test, analysis, formal, inspection, and provenance evidence; give it a canonical content identity; and state the narrow conclusion and non-proofs of each evidence type.
Compute claim-dependency, assumption, evidence, and trusted computing base closure while detecting missing references, excessive depth, cycles, and invalid composition arguments.
Derive deterministic fail-closed claim statuses and produce a calibrated, offline-replayable report with exact endpoints, deliberate failures, and residual-risk statements.

modules.title: Adversarial validation
modules.summary: Design tests that try to falsify parsers, implementations, proofs, policies, and artifacts.

outcomes.text: Turn scoped threat hypotheses into bounded structured, malformed, endpoint, and mutated cases with explicit lineage and canonical corpus identity.
Apply independent oracles, properties, metamorphic relations, and differential comparisons across parsers, implementations, proof or certificate checkers, policies, and artifacts.
Use implementation mutation and deterministic counterexample minimization to challenge a suite while preserving failures and restored passes without overstating coverage.
Report exact resource endpoints, exercised hypotheses, uncovered space, oracle and coverage limits, trust dependencies, residual risks, and calibrated assurance claims.

modules.title: Reproducibility, supply chains, and offline replay
modules.summary: Bind evidence to exact content and replay it without undeclared network trust.

outcomes.text: Design a canonical manifest binding subject, sequence, materials, dependency closure, provenance identities, environment, TCB, recipe, and expected result.
Validate hostile bounded replay bundles before material retention and safely isolate verified writes.
Replay authenticated dependency closure offline and detect substitution, omission, addition, rollback, provenance/TCB drift, and deterministic-output mismatch.
Produce reproducible, failure-sensitive evidence with exact identities, endpoints, environment/TCB observations, residual risks, and narrow non-claims.

modules.title: Release lifecycle and vulnerability response
modules.summary: Operate updates, withdrawals, incident containment, correction, and downstream communication.

outcomes.text: Define a versioned release lifecycle with explicit artifact identity, compatibility and support policy, update and migration rules, rollback prevention, retirement, revocation, and withdrawal behavior.
Operate a vulnerability response from bounded intake and triage through claim invalidation, containment, advisory, update or withdrawal, downstream notification, closure, and recovery.
Preserve canonical timelines and failure-sensitive evidence, exercise exact endpoints and illegal transitions, and run deterministic recovery drills whose results can be replayed offline.

Inventory the lifecycle trust boundary and state only claims supported by local records, distinguishing referenced actions from verified external execution and excluding unsupported Orange or production claims.

modules.title: Pin and run Orange Today
modules.summary: Identify the exact merged compiler revision, run the current CLI, and report the bounded S3a result without calling it compilation or proof.

outcomes.text: Verify the Orange repository revision and pinned Rust toolchain.
Run orangec check on a valid Orange 2026 source and interpret the silent success.
State at least four capabilities that the current check result does not establish.

modules.title: Orange 2026 lexical model
modules.summary: Predict tokens, trivia, spans, limits, and lexical failures for the implemented edition.

outcomes.text: Classify Orange 2026 identifiers, reserved words, integers, strings, comments, punctuation, and whitespace.
Use orangec lex to inspect deterministic tokens and byte spans.
Predict a lexical rejection and identify its stable diagnostic code.

modules.title: Orange 2026 grammar, diagnostics, and conformance
modules.summary: Author every currently accepted syntactic form and falsify acceptance at grammar boundaries.

outcomes.text: Write the complete current Orange 2026 grammar from its production rules.
Create accepted and rejected source cases that distinguish lexical, syntactic, and S3a semantic boundaries.
Scope recovered parse evidence separately from full check success and exclude unsupported execution, proof, compilation, and cryptographic claims.

modules.title: Orange S3a typed literals and reference evaluation
modules.summary: Check closed Int and Word[8] specification literals and interpret source-ordered reference values at the exact accepted pre-alpha S3a revision.

outcomes.text: Distinguish the separate spec and impl declaration namespaces, same-kind uniqueness, and the absence of a relation between same-spelled declarations.
Check closed typed specification literals against the exact Int and Word[8] normalization, range, and diagnostic rules.
Interpret the source-ordered noncanonical Typed Reference Core and predict exact orangec eval values.
Record pinned check and eval evidence without promoting it into unsupported language, proof, compilation, security, or release claims.

modules.title: Orange specifications and implementations
modules.summary: Author real specification and implementation bodies with contracts once the language defines them.
blocked_by: S3a defines only closed typed specification literals; parameters, operators, calls, bindings, control flow, dynamic failure, typed implementations, contracts, effects, and specification/implementation refinement remain undefined or unimplemented.

modules.title: Orange proofs, obligations, and claim matrices
modules.summary: Discharge separate proof obligations and classify mixed claims once S4 exists.
blocked_by: S3a's internal Typed Reference Core has no canonical encoding, proof identity, refinement relation, or proof-checking role; D-006 and Orange S4 remain pending.

modules.title: Orange games, secrecy, and leakage claims
modules.summary: Connect games, reductions, information flow, and exact leakage models once defined.
blocked_by: Orange defines no game, proof, effect, leakage, target, or constant-time semantics; S4 and S6 remain pending.

modules.title: Orange compiler and native artifact path
modules.summary: Build, inspect, and falsify assurance-preserving native artifact chains once implemented.
blocked_by: S3a has no lowering or target meaning; S5 and S6 provide no compiler IR, preservation relation, code generation, object path, or admitted target.

modules.title: Orange C and Rust integration
modules.summary: Generate, integrate, and attack-test foreign interfaces once Orange exposes an ABI.
blocked_by: S6 provides no layout, ownership or buffer, ABI, foreign-boundary, generated C or Rust, or supported target-tuple contract.

modules.title: Orange packages, evidence bundles, and offline replay
modules.summary: Assemble and replay hostile-input evidence bundles once package and release surfaces exist.
blocked_by: Orange has no S7 admitted cryptography corpus and no S8 manifest, lock, package, evidence-bundle, or offline-replay format or implementation.

modules.title: Orange update, withdrawal, and incident operations
modules.summary: Exercise profile updates, withdrawals, claim invalidation, and vulnerability response on real Orange releases.
blocked_by: The exact pin is a pre-alpha compiler slice, not a release; S8 provides no supported release, update, withdrawal, support-window, or incident-operation surface.

modules.title: Shared Orange professional studio
modules.summary: Complete the common standards-to-artifact-to-evidence workflow before specializing.
blocked_by: The shared studio requires runnable J-01 through J-08 Orange surfaces; S3 is incomplete and S4 through S8 are absent at the exact merged pre-alpha pin.

modules.title: Cryptographic implementation specialization
modules.summary: Deep practice in target-conscious implementation, benchmarking, refinement, and leakage evidence.
blocked_by: Orange has no typed implementation, proof or refinement, leakage or target, native-artifact, or ABI surface.

modules.title: Cryptographic implementer capstone
critical_criteria: exact standard, source, toolchain, and target identity
correct implementation with positive, negative, and adversarial evidence
precise refinement, leakage, performance, and artifact claim boundaries
modules.summary: Independently deliver and defend a standards-grounded, claim-scoped native cryptographic implementation.
blocked_by: No supported Orange professional release surface exists for a native, artifact-scoped cryptographic implementation.

modules.title: Verification engineering specialization
modules.summary: Deep practice in obligations, proof replay, automation boundaries, assumptions, and TCB closure.
blocked_by: Orange has no proof IR, checker, claim boundary, or package and evidence replay surface.

modules.title: Verification engineer capstone
critical_criteria: complete obligation, assumption, axiom, and trusted-base inventory
authoritative and independent replay with mutation failures at the correct boundary
precise separation of checked proof, automation, testing, and external evidence
modules.summary: Independently prove, replay, mutate, and defend a mixed Orange claim matrix.
blocked_by: No supported Orange professional release surface exists for proof, claim, trust-closure, or independent offline-replay evidence.

modules.title: Cryptography and standards specialization
modules.summary: Deep practice in standards transcription, games, parameters, provenance, and vectors.
blocked_by: Orange has no specification bodies beyond closed literals, games, proofs, admitted cryptography corpus, or package and release surface.

modules.title: Cryptographer and standards author capstone
critical_criteria: exact standards, errata, parameter, and rights provenance
faithful specification, vector, game, and assumption mapping
traceable change and withdrawal impact across dependent claims
modules.summary: Independently translate and defend a standards slice, specification, games, provenance, and vectors.
blocked_by: No supported Orange professional release surface exists for standards-to-specification, game, proof, provenance, and migration evidence.

modules.title: Library integration and maintenance specialization
modules.summary: Deep practice in C/Rust integration, misuse resistance, compatibility, dispatch, and updates.
blocked_by: Orange has no typed implementation, native or ABI, package, release, update, or withdrawal surface.

modules.title: Library maintainer and integrator capstone
critical_criteria: exact artifact, ABI, target, dispatch, and consumer identity
misuse-resistant success and fail-closed foreign-boundary behavior
reproducible release, update, withdrawal, and incident evidence
modules.summary: Independently integrate, attack-test, release, update, and defend an Orange library boundary.
blocked_by: No supported Orange professional release surface exists for ABI integration, dispatch, packaging, updates, or incident evidence.

modules.title: Audit and downstream assurance specialization
modules.summary: Deep practice in hostile-bundle inspection, independent replay, artifact tracing, and claim impact.
blocked_by: Orange has no native artifact chain, ABI, evidence bundle, offline replay, release, or incident surface.

modules.title: Auditor and downstream consumer capstone
critical_criteria: hostile-input bundle validation and exact artifact identity
offline proof, build, test, and trust-closure replay
precise invalidation, residual-risk, and downstream suitability report
modules.summary: Independently audit hostile inputs, replay evidence, mutate claims, and defend an exact trust report.
blocked_by: No supported Orange professional release surface exists for hostile-bundle, artifact, trust-closure, replay, or downstream invalidation evidence.
